3. To one who is devoid of attachment who is liberated whose mind is established in knowledge and who works for the sake of sacrifice for the sake of God the whole action is dissolved
4. The action is completely dissolved in the case of the person who undertakes it for the sake of sacrifice who is free from attachment and has been liberated and whose mind is fixed in wisdom
5. He who is without attachment free his mind centered in wisdom and his actions done as a sacrifice leave no trace behind
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.24 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.24 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: बरहमरपण बरहमहवरबरहमगन बरहमण हतम बरहमव तन गनतवय बरहमकरमसमधन 424
Translations:

1. Brahman is the instrument with which to offer Brahman is the oblation By Brahman is the oblation offered into the fire of Brahman Brahman alone is to be attained by one who meditates on Him in their works
2. The ladle is Brahman the oblations are Brahman and the offering is poured by Brahman into the fire of Brahman He who has concentration on Brahman as the objective can only reach Brahman
3. Brahman is the oblation Brahman is the melted butter ghee by Brahman is the oblation poured into the fire of Brahman Brahman indeed shall be attained by one who always sees Brahman in action

4. The Brahmanoblation that is to be offered to the Brahman is poured into the Brahmanfire by the Brahman it is nothing but the Brahman that is to be attained by one whose deep contemplation is the Brahmanaction

5. For him the sacrifice is the Spirit the Spirit and the offering are one it is the Spirit that is sacrificed in its own fire and the man even in action is united with God since his mind never ceases to be fixed on Him while performing the act
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.25 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.25 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: दवमवपर यजञ यगन परयपसत बरहमगनवपर यजञ यजञनवपजहवत 425
Translations:
1. Some yogis resort only to the sacrifice relating to gods others offer sacrifice into the fire of Brahman solely through sacrifice
2. Other yogis undertake sacrifice to gods alone while others offer the Self as a sacrifice by themselves in the fire of Brahman
3. Some yogis perform sacrifice to the gods alone while others who have realized the Self offer the Self as sacrifice in the fire of Brahman alone
4. Certain other men of Yoga are completely devoted to yajna connected with the devas and offer that yajna simply as a yajna into the insatiable fire of the Brahman
5. Some sages sacrifice to the powers while others offer themselves on the altar of the eternal
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.26 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.26 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शरतरदननदरयणयनय सयमगनष जहवत शबददनवषयननय इनदरयगनष जहवत 426
Translations:
1. Others offer as oblations hearing and other senses into the fires of restraint Some others offer as oblations the objects of the senses such as sound and the rest into the fires of their senses
2. Others offer their organs such as their ears etc in the fires of selfcontrol Others offer the objects such as sound etc in the fires of their organs
3. Some again offer the organ of hearing and other senses as a sacrifice in the fire of restraint others offer sound and other objects of the senses as a sacrifice in the fire of the senses
4. Others offer their senseorgans such as the sense of hearing and the rest into the fires of restraint others offer objects such as sound and the rest into the fires of their senseorgans
5. Some sacrifice their physical senses in the fire of selfcontrol others offer up their contact with external objects in the sacrificial fire of their senses
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.27 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.27 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सरवणनदरयकरमण परणकरमण चपर आतमसयमयगगन जहवत जञनदपत 427
Translations:
1. Some again offer as an oblation the functions of the senses and the activity of the vital breaths into the fire of the Yoga of restraint of the mind kindled by knowledge
2. Others offer all the activities of the organs and the activities of the vital force into the fire of the yoga of selfcontrol lit by Knowledge
3. Others again sacrifice all the functions of the senses and those of the breath vital energy or Prana in the fire of the Yoga of selfrestraint kindled by knowledge
4. Some others offer all the actions of their senseorgans and the actions of their lifebreath into the fire of the Yoga of selfcontrol ignited by wisdom
5. Others again sacrifice their activities and vitality in the spiritual fire of selfabnegation kindled by wisdom
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.28 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.28 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: दरवययजञसतपयजञ यगयजञसतथपर सवधययजञनयजञशच यतय सशतवरत 428
Translations:
1. Selfcontrolled and firm of resolve others perform the sacrifice of material objects or austerities or yoga while others offer their scriptural study and knowledge
2. Similarly others are performers of sacrifices through wealth through austerity through yoga and through study and knowledge others are ascetics with severe vows
3. Others again offer wealth austerity and Yoga as sacrifice while ascetics of selfrestraint and rigid vows offer the study of scriptures and knowledge as sacrifice

4. These are the performers of sacrifices with material objects the performers of sacrifices with penance and the performers of sacrifices with Yoga Likewise there are other ascetics with rigid vows whose sacrifices are the svadhyayaknowledge
5. Others offer as their sacrifice wealth austerities and meditation Monks wedded to their vows renounce their scriptural learning and even their spiritual powers
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.29 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.29 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: अपन जहवत परण परणऽपन तथऽपर परणपनगत रदधव परणयमपरयण 429
Translations:

1. Others with restricted diets are devoted to the control of breath Some sacrifice the inward breath in the outward breath similarly others sacrifice the outward breath in the inward breath Some others stopping the flow of both the inward and outward breaths sacrifice the inward and outward breaths

2. Constantly practicing control of the vital forces by stopping the movements of the outgoing and incoming breaths some offer as a sacrifice the outgoing breath into the incoming breath while still others the incoming breath into the outgoing breath

3. Others offer as sacrifice the outgoing breath into the incoming and the incoming into the outgoing restraining the flow of the outgoing and the incoming breaths solely absorbed in the restraint of the breath

4. Some sages offer the prana into the apana likewise others offer the apana into the prana Having controlled both the courses of the prana and apana the same sages with their desires fulfilled by the above activities and with their food restricted offer the pranas into pranas All these persons know what sacrifices are and have their sins destroyed by them
5. There are some who practice controlling the vital energy and governing the subtle forces of prana and apana thereby sacrificing their prana to apana or their apana to prana
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.30 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.30 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: अपर नयतहर परणनपरणष जहवत सरवऽपयत यजञवद यजञकषपतकलमष 430
Translations:
1. All these know the meaning of sacrifices and through sacrifices their sins are eradicated Those who subsist on the ambrosial food the remnants of sacrifices go to eternal Brahman
2. Others having their food regulated offer the vital forces in the vital forces All of them are knowers of the sacrifice and have their sins destroyed by the sacrifice
3. Others who regulate their diet offer lifebreaths in each lifebreath All these are knowers of sacrifice whose sins are destroyed through sacrifice

4. Some sages offer the prana into the apana likewise others offer the apana into the prana Having controlled both the courses of the prana and apana the same sages with their desires fulfilled by the above activities and with their food restricted offer the pranas into pranas All these persons know what sacrifices are and have their sins destroyed by them
5. Others controlling their diet sacrifice their worldly life to the spiritual fire All understand the principle of sacrifice and by it their sins are washed away
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.31 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.31 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यजञशषटमतभज यनत बरहम सनतनम नय लकऽसतययजञसय कतऽनय करसततम 431
Translations:
1. This world is not for one who makes no sacrifice how then for the other O Arjuna

2. Those who partake of the nectar left over after a sacrifice reach the eternal Brahman This world ceases to exist for one who does not perform sacrifices what then to speak of the other world O best among the Kurus Arjuna
3. Those who eat the remnants of the sacrifice which are like nectar go to the eternal Brahman This world is not for the one who does not perform sacrifice how then can they have the other O Arjuna
4. The eaters of the sacrificeordained nectar attain the eternal Brahman This world is not for a nonsacrificer how can there be the other O best of the Kurus
5. Tasting the nectar of immortality as the reward of sacrifice they reach the Eternal This world is not for those who refuse to sacrifice nor is the other world
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.32 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.32 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: एव बहवध यजञ वतत बरहमण मख करमजनवदध तनसरवनव जञतव वमकषयस 432
Translations:
1. Thus many forms of sacrifices have been spread out as means of reaching Brahman individual self in its own nature Know that all of these are born of actions Knowing thus you will be free
2. Thus various kinds of sacrifices are spread at the mouth of the Vedas Know them all to be born of action Knowing this you will become liberated
3. Thus manifold sacrifices are spread out before Brahman at the face of Brahman Know them all to be born of action and thus knowing you shall be liberated
4. Thus sacrifices of many varieties have been elaborated upon by the mouth of the Brahman Know them all as having sprung from actions By knowing thus you shall be liberated
5. In this way other sacrifices may also be undertaken for the sake of the Spirit Know that they all depend on action Knowing this you will be free
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.33 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.33 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शरयनदरवयमयदयजञजजञनयजञ परनतप सरव करमखल परथ जञन परसमपयत 433
Translations:
1. The sacrifice of knowledge is superior to material sacrifice O Arjuna all actions and everything else culminate in knowledge
2. O destroyer of enemies knowledge considered as a sacrifice is greater than sacrifices requiring materials O son of Prtha all actions in their totality culminate in knowledge
3. Superior is wisdomsacrifice to the sacrifice with objects O Parantapa scorcher of the foes All actions in their entirety O Arjuna culminate in knowledge
4. The sacrifice of knowledge is superior to the sacrifice of material things O son of Prtha destroyer of enemies All actions leaving nothing behind come to an end in knowledge
5. The sacrifice of wisdom is superior to any material sacrifice for O Arjuna the pinnacle of action is always realization
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.34 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.34 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: तदवदध परणपतन परपरशनन सवय उपदकषयनत त जञन जञननसतततवदरशन 434
Translations:
1. Know this through prostration questioning and service The wise who have realized the truth will instruct you in knowledge
2. Know that the wise ones who have realized the Truth will impart the Knowledge to you through prostration inquiry and service
3. Know that the wise who have realized the truth will instruct thee in that knowledge through long prostration supplication and service

4. Learn this from those endowed with knowledge through prostration inquiry and service offered to them Those who are endowed with knowledge and are capable of showing the truth will give you the truth nearby
5. This you shall learn by prostrating yourself at the masters feet questioning them and serving them The wise who have realized the truth will teach you wisdom
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.35 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.35 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यजजञतव न पनरमहमव यसयस पणडव यन भतनयशषण दरकषयसयतमनयथ मय 435
Translations:
1. Knowing this O Arjuna you will not fall again into delusion in this wayby that knowledge you will see all beings without exception in yourself and then in Me
2. Knowing this O Pandava you will not be deluded again in this way and through it you will see all beings without exception in the Self and also in Me
3. Knowing that thou shalt not O Arjuna again be deluded like this and by that thou shalt see all beings in thyself and also in me
4. By knowing this you will not be deluded again O son of Pandu and by this means you will see all beings without exception in yourself that is in Me
5. Having known That you shall never again be confounded and O Arjuna by the power of that wisdom you shall see all these people as if they were your own Self and therefore as Me
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.36 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.36 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: अप चदस पपभय सरवभय पपकततम सरव जञनपलवनव वजन सनतरषयस 436
Translations:
1. Even if you are the most sinful of all sinners you can cross over all sins by the boat of knowledge alone
2. Even if you are the worst sinner among all sinners you can still cross over all wickedness with the raft of Knowledge alone
3. Even if thou art the most sinful of all sinners yet thou shalt surely cross over all sins by the raft of knowledge
4. Even if you are the highest sinner among all sinners you can cross over the ocean of all sins by the boat of knowledge
5. Even if you are the greatest of sinners you will cross over all sin by the ferryboat of wisdom
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.37 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.37 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यथधस समदधऽगनरभसमसतकरतऽरजन जञनगन सरवकरमण भसमसतकरत तथ 437
Translations:
1. Just as a burning fire turns fuel to ashes O Arjuna so does the fire of knowledge turn all karmas to ashes
2. O Arjuna just as a blazing fire reduces wood to ashes similarly the fire of Knowledge reduces all actions to ashes
3. As the blazing fire reduces fuel to ashes O Arjuna so does the fire of knowledge reduce all actions to ash
4. Just as a fire that is wellinflamed reduces fuel to ashes so too does the fire of knowledge reduce all actions to ashes
5. As the kindled fire consumes the fuel so O Arjuna the embers of action are burnt to ashes in the flame of wisdom
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.38 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.38 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: न ह जञनन सदश पवतरमह वदयत ततसवय यगससदध कलनतमन वनदत 438
Translations:
1. For there is no purifier equal to knowledge he who is perfected in Karma Yoga finds this knowledge of his own accord in himself in due time
2. Indeed there is nothing purifying here comparable to Knowledge One who has become perfected over time through yoga realizes That in their own heart
3. Verily there is no purifier in this world like knowledge He who is perfected in Yoga finds it within the Self in due time
4. In this world there exists no purifier comparable to knowledge One who becomes perfect in Yoga finds this of their own accord in their Self in due course
5. There is nothing in the world so purifying as wisdom and he who is a perfect saint finds that at last within himself
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.39 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.39 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शरदधवललभत जञन ततपर सयतनदरय जञन लबधव पर शनतमचरणधगचछत 439
Translations:
1. He who has faith is intent on it and has mastered his senses attains knowledge Having attained knowledge he quickly goes to supreme peace
2. The one who has faith is diligent and has control over their organs attains Knowledge Achieving Knowledge one soon attains supreme Peace
3. The one who is full of faith devoted to it and has subdued their senses obtains this knowledge and upon obtaining the knowledge they attain the supreme peace immediately
4. He who has faith gains knowledge if he is solely intent upon it and has his senseorgans wellcontrolled Having gained the knowledge he soon attains the Supreme Peace
5. He who is full of faith attains wisdom and he who can control his senses having attained that wisdom will soon attain Supreme Peace
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.40 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.40 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: अजञशचशरददधनशच सशयतम वनशयत नय लकऽसत न पर न सख सशयतमन 440
Translations:
1. The ignorant the faithless and the doubting one perish for the doubting one there is neither this world nor the one beyond nor happiness
2. One who is ignorant and faithless and has a doubting mind perishes Neither this world nor the next nor happiness exists for one who has a doubting mind
3. The ignorant the faithless and the doubting self go to destruction there is neither this world nor the other nor happiness for the doubting one
4. But he who is ignorant and has no faith perishes with his mind full of doubts Neither this world nor the other nor happiness is for a person who is by nature full of doubts
5. But the ignorant man and he who has no faith and the skeptic are lost Neither in this world nor elsewhere is there any happiness in store for him who always doubts
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.41 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.41 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यगसनयसतकरमण जञनसछननसशयम आतमवनत न करमण नबधननत धनञजय 441
Translations:
1. Actions do not bind him O Arjuna who has renounced them through Karma Yoga and whose doubts have been dispelled by knowledge and who thus possesses a steady mind
2. O Dhananjaya Arjuna actions do not bind one who has renounced actions through yoga whose doubts have been fully dispelled by Knowledge and who is not inattentive
3. He who has renounced actions through Yoga whose doubts have been dispelled by knowledge and who is selfpossessedsuch a one is not bound by actions O Arjuna
4. O Dhananjaya Actions do not bind him who has renounced all actions through Yoga who has cut off his doubts by the sword of knowledge and who is a master of his own self
5. But the one who has renounced their actions for meditation who has cleaved their doubts in two by the sword of wisdom and who remains always enthroned in their Self is not bound by their actions
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 4.42 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 4.42 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: तसमदजञनसभत हतसथ जञनसनऽऽतमन छततवन सशय यगमतषठततषठ भरत 442
Translations:
1. Therefore sever with the sword of knowledge this doubt present in your heart resulting from ignorance concerning the Self Practice this Yoga O Arjuna and arise
2. Therefore O scion of the Bharata dynasty take recourse to yoga and rise up cutting asunder with the sword of knowledge this doubt in your heart arising from ignorance
3. Therefore with the sword of knowledge of the Self cut asunder the doubt of the self born of ignorance residing in your heart and take refuge in Yoga Arise O Arjuna
4. Therefore thus cutting off by means of the sword of knowledge the doubt that has sprung from ignorance and exists in your heart practice the Yoga Stand up O descendant of Bharata
5. Therefore cleave asunder with the sword of wisdom the doubts of the heart which your own ignorance has engendered and follow the path of wisdom and arise
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.1 spoken by अर्जुन. [/INST] Verse 5.1 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by अर्जुन, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सनयस करमण कषण पनरयग च शसस यचछरय एतयरक तनम बरह सनशचतम 51
Translations:
1. Arjuna said O Krsna you praise the renunciation of actions and then you also praise Karma Yoga Please tell me with certainty which of these is the superior one leading to the ultimate good
2. Arjuna said O Krsna you praise renunciation of actions and at the same time Karmayoga Please tell me for certain which one is better between these two
3. Arjuna said O Krishna you praise renunciation of actions and also yoga Please tell me conclusively which is better of the two
4. Arjuna said O Krsna you commend both renunciation of action and the Yoga of action which one of these two is superior Please tell me that for certain
5. Arjuna said My Lord At one moment You praise renunciation of action at another You praise right action Tell me truly I pray which of these is more conducive to my highest welfare
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.2 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.2 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सनयस करमयगशच नशरयसकरवभ तयसत करमसनयसतकरमयग वशषयत 52
Translations:
1. The Lord said Renunciation of actions and Karma Yoga both lead to the highest excellence however of the two Karma Yoga surpasses renunciation of actions
2. The Blessed Lord said Both renunciation of actions and Karmayoga lead to liberation however Karmayoga excels over renunciation of actions
3. The Blessed Lord said Renunciation and the Yoga of action both lead to the highest bliss but of the two the Yoga of action is superior to the renunciation of action
4. The Bhagavat said Both renunciation and the Yoga of action effect salvation however of these two the Yoga of action is superior to renunciation of action
5. Lord Shri Krishna replied Both renunciation of action and the path of right action lead to the highest however right action is the better of the two
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.3 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.3 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: जञय स नतयसनयस य न दवषट न कङकषत नरदवनदव ह महबह सख बनधतपरमचयत 53
Translations:
1. He who neither hates nor desires and is beyond the pairs of opposites is to be understood as an everrenouncer Therefore he is easily set free from bondage O Arjuna
2. He who neither hates nor craves should be known as a man of constant renunciation
3. He should be known as a perpetual Sannyasi who neither hates nor desires for free from the pairs of opposites O mightyarmed Arjuna he is easily freed from bondage

4. That person may be considered a man of permanent renunciation who neither hates nor desires For O mightyarmed one He who is free from the pairs of opposites is easily released from bondage of action
5. He is a true ascetic who never desires nor dislikes who is unaffected by the opposites and is easily freed from bondage
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.4 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.4 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सखययग पथगबल परवदनत न पणडत एकमपयसथत समयगभयरवनदत फलम 54
Translations:
1. Children not the learned speak of Sankhya and Yoga as distinct however he who is firmly set in one attains the fruit of both
2. The fools not the learned ones speak of Sankhya and Karma yoga as being different Anyone who properly resorts to either one of them gets the results of both
3. Children not the wise speak of knowledge and the Yoga of action or the performance of action as though they are distinct and different he who is truly established in one obtains the fruits of both
4. The childish and not the wise proclaim the paths of knowledge and Yoga to be different He who has properly resorted to even one of these two gets the fruit of both
5. Only the unenlightened speak of wisdom and right action as separate the wise do not If anyone knows one they enjoy the fruit of both
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.5 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.5 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यतसखय परपयत सथन तदयगरप गमयत एक सखय च यग च य पशयत स पशयत 55
Translations:

1. That state which is reached by the Sankhyans the same is also attained by the Yogins that is the same state is achieved by those who are Karma Yogins He alone is wise who perceives that the Sankhya and the Yoga are one and the same due to their having the same result
2. The state that is reached by the Sankhyas and that is also reached by the yogis is seen by one who sees Sankhya and yoga as one
3. That place which is reached by the Sankhyas or the Jnanis is also reached by the Yogis Karma Yogis He who sees knowledge and the performance of action Karma Yoga as one sees truly
4. Those who are knowledgeable reach the same state as those who practice Yoga so whoever sees the knowledgepath and the Yoga as one sees correctly
5. The level which is attained through wisdom is also reached through right action He who perceives that the two are one knows the truth
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.6 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.6 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सनयससत महबह दखमपतमयगत यगयकत मनरबरहम नचरणधगचछत 56
Translations:
1. But O mightyarmed one renunciation is hard to attain without practicing Yoga The sage who contemplates and follows Yoga soon reaches the Brahman the self or Atman
2. But O mightyarmed one renunciation is hard to attain without Karma yoga The meditative man equipped with yoga attains Brahman without delay
3. But O mightyarmed Arjuna renunciation is hard to attain without Yoga the sage who is in harmony with Yoga quickly goes to Brahman
4. O mightyarmed Arjuna Renunciation is indeed hard to attain except through Yoga the sage who is the master of Yoga quickly attains the Brahman
5. Without concentration O Mighty One renunciation is difficult But the sage who is always meditating on the Divine shall soon attain the Absolute
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.7 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.7 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यगयकत वशदधतम वजततम जतनदरय सरवभततमभततम करवननप न लपयत 57
Translations:
1. He who follows the Yoga and is pure in mind who has subdued his self and has conquered his senses and whose self has become the self of all beings even while he is acting remains untainted

2. Endowed with yoga pure in mind controlled in body a conqueror of the organs the Self of the selves of all beingshe does not become tainted even while performing actions When this person resorts to nitya and naimittika rites and duties as a means to the achievement of full illumination and thus becomes fully enlightened then even when he acts through the apparent functions of the mind organs etc he does not become affected
3. He who is devoted to the path of action whose mind is pure who has conquered the self who has subdued his senses and who realizes his Self as the Self in all beings though acting is not tainted

4. A master of yoga whose self mind and intellect is very pure the senseorgans are controlled and the soul is realized to be the soul of all beingshe is not stained even though he is a performer of actions
5. He who is spiritual pure has overcome his senses and his personal self and has realized his highest Self as the Self of alleven though he acts he is not bound by his actions
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.8 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.8 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: नव कचतकरमत यकत मनयत तततववत पशयन शरणवनसपशञजघरननशननगचछनसवपन शवसन 58
Translations:
1. The one who knows the truth and is devoted to yoga should think I am not doing anything even though they are seeing hearing touching smelling eating moving sleeping and breathing

2. Remaining absorbed in the Self the knower of Reality should think I certainly do not do anything even while seeing hearing touching smelling eating moving sleeping breathing speaking releasing holding opening and closing the eyesremembering that the organs function in relation to the objects of the organs
3. I do nothing at all thus would the harmonized knower of Truth think seeing hearing touching smelling eating going sleeping and breathing
4. A master of Yoga knowing the reality would think I do not perform any action at all For he who while seeing hearing touching smelling eating going sleeping and breathing
5. Though the saint sees hears touches smells eats moves sleeps and breathes yet he knows the truth and knows that it is not he who acts
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.9 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.9 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: परलपनवसजनगहणनननमषननमषननप इनदरयणनदरयरथष वरतनत इत धरयन 59
Translations:
1. Speaking discharging grasping opening and closing his eyes etc he should always bear in mind that the senses operate among senseobjects

2. Remaining absorbed in the Self the knower of Reality should think I certainly do not do anything even while seeing hearing touching smelling eating moving sleeping breathing speaking releasing holding opening and closing the eyesremembering that the organs function in relation to the objects of the organs
3. Speaking letting go seizing opening and closing the eyes one should be convinced that the senses move among the senseobjects
4. Taking rejecting receiving opening and closing the eyes bear in mind that the senseorgans are on their respective objects and
5. Though he talks gives receives opens his eyes and shuts them he still knows that his senses are merely disporting themselves among the objects of perception
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.10 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.10 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: बरहमणयधय करमण सङग तयकतव करत य लपयत न स पपन पदमपतरमवमभस 510
Translations:
1. He who acts without attachment reposing all actions on Brahman Prakṛti is untouched by evil just as a lotus leaf is untouched by water
2. One who acts by dedicating their actions to Brahman and by renouncing attachment they do not become polluted by sin just as a lotus leaf is not by water
3. He who does actions offering them to Brahman and abandoning attachment is not tainted by sin just as a lotus leaf is not tainted by water
4. Who performs actions by offering them to the Brahman and giving up attachmentthey are not stained by sin just as a lotus leaf is not stained by water
5. He who dedicates his actions to the Spirit without any personal attachment to them is no more tainted by sin than the water lily is wetted by water
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.11 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.11 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: कयन मनस बदधय कवलरनदरयरप यगन करम करवनत सङग तयकतवऽऽतमशदधय 511
Translations:
1. Yogins renouncing attachment do actions with merely the body mind intellect and senses for the purification of the self
2. By giving up attachment the yogis undertake work solely through the body mind intellect and even the organs for their own purification
3. Yogis having abandoned attachment perform actions only through the body mind intellect and even the senses for the purification of the self
4. Having given up attachment the men of Yoga perform action just with the body with the mind with intellect and also with their senseorgans for attaining the Self
5. The sage performs his action dispassionately using his body mind intellect and even his senses always as a means for purification
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.12 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.12 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यकत करमफल तयकतव शनतमपनत नषठकम अयकत कमकरण फल सकत नबधयत 512
Translations:
1. A yogi renouncing the fruits of their actions attains lasting peace But the unsteady person who is attached to the fruits of their actions being driven by desire is bound

2. Giving up the results of work by becoming resolute in faith one attains peace arising from steadfastness One who is lacking in resolute faith being attached to the results under the impulse of desire becomes bound

3. The one who is united the wellpoised or harmonized having abandoned the fruit of action attains eternal peace whereas the one who is not united the unsteady or unbalanced impelled by desire and attached to the fruit is bound

4. Having abandoned attachment to the fruit of actions the master of Yoga attains the highest Peace But one who is not a master of Yoga and is attached to the fruit of action is bound by their actions born of desire

5. Having abandoned the fruit of action he wins eternal peace Others unacquainted with spirituality led by desire and clinging to the benefit they think will follow their actions become entangled in them
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.13 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.13 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सरवकरमण मनस सनयसयसत सख वश नवदवर पर दह नव करवनन करयन 513
Translations:
1. The embodied self mentally resigning all actions as belonging to the city of nine gates ie the body and becoming selfcontrolled dwells happily neither acting himself nor causing the body to act
2. The embodied man of selfcontrol having given up all actions mentally continues happily in the city of nine gates without doing or causing anyone else to do anything
3. Mentally renouncing all actions and being selfcontrolled the embodied one happily rests in the ninegated city neither acting nor causing others body and senses to act
4. Having renounced all actions by mind a man of selfcontrol dwells happily in his body a ninewindowed mansion neither performing nor causing others to perform any actions
5. Mentally renouncing all actions the selfcontrolled soul enjoys bliss in this body the city of the nine gates neither doing anything themselves nor causing anything to be done
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.14 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.14 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: न करततव न करमण लकसय सजत परभ न करमफलसयग सवभवसत परवरतत 514
Translations:
1. The Lord of the body the Self ie the Jiva does not create agency nor actions nor union with the fruits of actions in relation to the world of selves only the inherent tendencies function
2. The Self does not create agency or any objects of desire for anyone nor does it associate with the results of actions rather it is Nature that acts
3. Neither does the Lord create agency nor actions for the world nor union with the fruits of actions rather it is Nature that acts
4. The Lord Self acquires neither the state of being a creator of the world nor the actions nor the connection with the fruits of their actions But it is the inherent nature in It that exerts
5. The Lord of this universe has not ordained activity nor any incentive thereto nor any relation between an act and its consequences all this is the work of Nature
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.15 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.15 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: नदतत कसयचतपप न चव सकत वभ अजञननवत जञन तन महयनत जनतव 515
Translations:
1. The allpervasive One takes away neither the sin nor the merit of anyone Knowledge is enveloped by ignorance and creatures are thereby deluded
2. The Omnipresent neither accepts anyones sin nor even virtue knowledge remains covered by ignorance thus the creatures become deluded
3. The Lord takes neither the demerit nor the merit of any knowledge is enveloped by ignorance and beings are deluded
4. The Omnimanifest Soul takes neither sin nor merit born of any action upon itself But perfect knowledge is clouded by Illusion and thus the creatures are deluded
5. The Lord does not accept responsibility for any persons sin or merit they are deluded for wisdom is submerged in ignorance within them
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.16 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.16 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: जञनन त तदजञन यष नशतमतमन तषमदतयवजजञन परकशयत ततपरम 516
Translations:
1. But for those in whom this ignorance is destroyed by the knowledge of the Self that knowledge in their case is supreme and shines brightly like the sun
2. But in the case of those whose ignorance is destroyed by knowledge of the Self their knowledge like the sun reveals that supreme Reality
3. But to those whose ignorance is destroyed by knowledge of the Self like the sun knowledge reveals the Supreme Brahman
4. In the case of those whose illusion has been destroyed by Selfknowledge that knowledge illuminates itself like the sun
5. Surely wisdom is like the sun revealing the supreme truth to those whose ignorance has been dispelled by the wisdom of the Self
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.17 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.17 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: तदबदधयसतदतमनसतननषठसततपरयण गचछनतयपनरवतत जञननरधतकलमष 517
Translations:

1. Those whose intellects pursue it whose minds think about it who undergo discipline for it and who hold it as their highest object have their impurities cleansed by knowledge and go from whence there is no return

2. Those whose intellect is absorbed in That whose Self is That who are steadfast in That and who have That as their supreme Goalthey attain the state of nonreturning their impurities having been removed by Knowledge
3. Their intellect absorbed in That their self being That established in That with That as their supreme goal they go whence there is no return their sins dispelled by knowledge

4. Those who have their intellect and self mind devoted to This who have established themselves in This and have This alone as their supreme goal and who have washed off their sins through perfect knowledgethey reach a state from which there is no more return
5. Meditating on the divine having faith in the divine concentrating on the divine and losing themselves in the divine their sins dissolve in wisdom and they go from whence there is no return
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.18 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.18 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: वदयवनयसपनन बरहमण गव हसतन शन चव शवपक च पणडत समदरशन 518
Translations:
1. The sages look with an equal eye on one endowed with learning and humility a Brahmana a cow an elephant a dog and a dogeater
2. The learned ones look with equanimity on a Brahmana endowed with learning and humility a cow an elephant and even a dog as well as one who eats dog meat
3. Sages look with an equal eye on a Brahmana endowed with learning and humility on a cow an elephant a dog and even an outcaste
4. The wise by nature look equally upon a Brahmana rich in learning and humility a cow an elephant and even a dog and a dogcooker an outcaste
5. Sages look upon all equally whether they be a minister of learning and humility an infidel a cow an elephant or a dog
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.19 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.19 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: इहव तरजत सरग यष समय सथत मन नरदष ह सम बरहम तसमदबरहमण त सथत 519
Translations:
1. Here itself those whose minds rest in equanimity overcome samsara For the Brahman individual self when uncontaminated by Prakriti is the same everywhere therefore they abide in Brahman
2. Here itself is mirth attained by those whose minds are established in sameness Since Brahman is the same in all and free from defects they are established in Brahman
3. Even here in this world those whose minds rest in reality overcome birth Brahman is indeed spotless and real therefore they are established in Brahman
4. The one who knows Brahman who is disillusioned established in Brahman and has a firm intellect would neither rejoice upon meeting a friend nor get agitated upon meeting a foe
5. Even in this world those whose minds remain always balanced fixed on the Supreme conquer their earthly life for the Supreme has neither blemish nor bias
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.20 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.20 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: न परहषयतपरय परपय नदवजतपरपय चपरयम सथरबदधरसममढ बरहमवदबरहमण सथत 520
Translations:

1. He who knows Brahman individual self and abides in Brahman whose mind is steadfastly focused on the self and undeluded by body consciousnesshe neither rejoices at gaining what is pleasant nor grieves on obtaining what is unpleasant

2. A knower of Brahman who is established in Brahman should have their intellect steady and should not be deluded They should not get delighted by getting what is desirable nor become dejected by getting what is undesirable
3. Resting in Brahman with a steady intellect and undeluded the knower of Brahman neither rejoices upon obtaining what is pleasant nor grieves upon obtaining what is unpleasant
4. He who with his mind not attached to external contacts finds happiness in the Selfthat person with his mind engaged in Yoga easily pervades the Brahman suffering no loss
5. He who knows and lives in the Absolute remains unmoved and unperturbed he is not elated by pleasure nor depressed by pain
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.21 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.21 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: बहयसपरशषवसकततम वनदतयतमन यतसखम स बरहमयगयकततम सखमकषयमशनत 521
Translations:
1. He whose mind is detached from external contact and finds happiness in the Selfhe has his mind engaged in the contemplation of Brahman and enjoys undecaying bliss
2. With his heart unattached to external objects he gets the bliss that is in the Self With his heart absorbed in meditation on Brahman he acquires undying Bliss
3. With the self unattached to external contacts he finds happiness in the Self with the self engaged in the meditation of Brahman he attains endless happiness

4. The enjoyments that arise from contact with objects are nothing but sources of misery having both a beginning and an end Therefore an intelligent person does not take delight in them O son of Kunti
5. He finds happiness in his own Self and enjoys eternal bliss whose heart does not yearn for the contacts of the earth and whose Self is one with the Everlasting
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.22 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.22 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: य ह ससपरशज भग दखयनय एव त आदयनतवनत कनतय न तष रमत बध 522
Translations:
1. For those pleasures that are born of contact are wombs of pain They have a beginning and an end O Arjuna the wise do not rejoice in them
2. Since enjoyments that result from contact with objects are indeed the sources of sorrow having a beginning and an end O son of Kunti the wise one does not delight in them
3. The enjoyments that arise from contact are only sources of pain for they have a beginning and an end O Arjuna the wise do not rejoice in them
4. Whosoever right here before abandoning the body is capable of bearing the force sprung from desire and wrathhe is considered to be a man of Yoga and a happy man
5. The joys that spring from external associations bring pain they have their beginnings and their endings The wise person does not rejoice in them
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.23 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.23 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शकनतहव य सढ परकशररवमकषणत कमकरधदभव वग स यकत स सख नर 523
Translations:
1. He who is able even here before being released from the body to bear the impulse generated by desire and anger he is a Yogin competent for selfrealisation he is the happy man
2. One who can withstand here itself before departing from the body the impulse arising from desire and anger that person is a yogi they are happy
3. He who is able while still here in this world to withstand the impulse born out of desire and anger before the liberation from the body he is a Yogi and he is a happy man
4. He whose pleasure delight and light are all withinO son of Prthahe attains the supreme Yoga becoming the Brahman himself
5. He who before leaving his body learns to overcome the promptings of desire and anger is a saint and is happy
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.24 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.24 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यऽनतसखऽनतररमसतथनतरजयतरव य स यग बरहमनरवण बरहमभतऽधगचछत 524
Translations:
1. He whose joy is within whose pleasure is within and whose light is withinhe is a yogin who having become Brahman attains the bliss of Brahman
2. One who is content within whose pleasure is within and whose light is only within that yogi having become Brahman attains absorption into Brahman
3. He who is happy within who rejoices within and who is illuminated within that Yogi attains absolute freedom or Moksha becoming Brahman himself
4. The seers whose doubts have decayed by whom dualities have been cut off whose minds are controlled and who delight in the welfare of all they attain the Brahman the Transcendent One
5. He who is happy within himself and has found its peace and in whom the inner light shines that sage attains eternal bliss and becomes one with the spirit
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.25 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.25 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: लभनत बरहमनरवणमषय कषणकलमष छननदवध यततमन सरवभतहत रत 525
Translations:
1. The sages who are free from the pairs of opposites whose minds are well subdued and who are devoted to the welfare of all beings become cleansed of all impurities and attain the bliss of Brahman
2. The seers whose sins have been attenuated who are free from doubt whose senses are under control who are engaged in doing good to all beings attain absorption in Brahman
3. The sages obtain absolute freedom or Moksha when their sins have been destroyed their dualities have been torn asunder they are selfcontrolled and they are intent on the welfare of all beings
4. At all times there is the transcendent Brahman for the ascetics who have severed their connection with desire and anger who have controlled their minds and have realized their Self
5. Sages whose sins have been washed away whose sense of separateness has dissipated who have subdued themselves and seek only the welfare of all come to the Eternal Spirit
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.26 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.26 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: कमकरधवयकतन यतन यतचतसम अभत बरहमनरवण वरतत वदततमनम 526
Translations:
1. To those who are free from desire and wrath who are accustomed to exerting themselves whose thoughts are controlled and who have conquered itthe beatitude of Brahman is close at hand
2. To those monks who have control over their internal organs who are free from desire and anger who have known the Self there is absorption into Brahman either way
3. Absolute freedom exists on all sides for those selfcontrolled ascetics who are free from desire and anger who have controlled their thoughts and who have realized the Self
4. Warding off external contacts keeping the sense of sight between the two wandering ones counterbalancing both the forward and backward moving forces that travel within what is crooked
5. Saints who know themselves who control their minds and feel neither desire nor anger find eternal bliss everywhere
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.27 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.27 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सपरशनकतव बहरबहयशचकषशचवनतर भरव परणपन सम कतव नसभयनतरचरण 527
Translations:
1. Shutting off outward contacts fixing the gaze between the eyebrows realizing inward and outward breaths moving in the nostrils

2. Keeping external objects outside fixing the eyes at the juncture of the eyebrows and controlling the outgoing and incoming breaths that move through the nostrils the contemplative who has mastery over his organs mind and intellect should be fully intent on Liberation and free from desire fear and anger He who is ever thus is verily free
3. Shutting out all external contacts and fixing the gaze between the eyebrows realizing the outgoing and incoming breaths moving within the nostrils
4. The sage who has controlled his senseorgans mind and intellect whose chief aim is emancipation and from whom desire fear and wrath have departedhe remains ever free
5. Excluding external objects with his gaze fixed between the eyebrows and the inward and outward breathings passing equally through his nostrils
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.28 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.28 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यतनदरयमनबदधरमनरमकषपरयण वगतचछभयकरध य सद मकत एव स 528
Translations:
1. The sage who has controlled his senses mind and intellect who is intent on release as his final goal freed forever from desire fear and wrath is indeed liberated forever

2. Keeping external objects outside fixing the eyes at the juncture of the eyebrows and controlling the outgoing and incoming breaths that move through the nostrils the contemplative who has mastery over his organs mind and intellect should be fully intent on Liberation and free from desire fear and anger He who is ever thus is verily free
3. With the senses mind and intellect ever controlled having liberation as their supreme goal free from desire fear and anger the sage is truly liberated forever
4. Having known Me as the Enjoyer of the fruits of sacrifices and austerities as the great Lord of all the worlds and as the Friend of all beings he attains peace
5. Governing sense mind and intellect intent on liberation free from desire fear and anger the sage is eternally free
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 5.29 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 5.29 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: भकतर यजञतपस सरवलकमहशवरम सहद सरवभतन जञतव म शनतमचछत 529
Translations:
1. Knowing Me as the enjoyer of all sacrifices and austerities as the Supreme Lord of all worlds as the Friend of every being he attains peace
2. One attains peace by knowing Me who am the great Lord of all the worlds the enjoyer of sacrifices and austerities and the friend of all creatures
3. He who knows Me as the enjoyer of sacrifices and austerities the great Lord of all the worlds and the friend of all beings attains peace
4. There is no such translation for this shloka
5. Knowing Me as the One who gladly receives all offerings of austerity and sacrifice as the Mighty Ruler of all the Worlds and the Friend of all beings he passes to Eternal Peace
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.1 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.1 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: अनशरत करमफल करय करम करत य स सनयस च यग च न नरगनरन चकरय 61
Translations:
1. The Lord said He who performs works that ought to be done without seeking their fruitshe is a sannyasin and yogin and not he who maintains no sacred fires and performs no actions
2. The Blessed Lord said He who performs an action which is his duty without depending on the result of the action is a monk and a yogi not so in he who does not keep afire and is actionless
3. The Blessed Lord said He who performs his bounden duty without depending on the fruits of his actionshe is a sannyasi and a yogi not he who is without fire and without action
4. The Bhagavad said He who performs his bounden action without depending on its fruit is the man of renunciation and the man of Yoga not he who simply remains without his fires and actions
5. Lord Shri Krishna said He who acts out of duty without considering the consequences is truly spiritual and a true ascetic not he who merely observes rituals or shuns all action
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.2 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.2 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: य सनयसमत परहरयग त वदध पणडव न हयसनयसतसङकलप यग भवत कशचन 62
Translations:

1. That which is called Sannyasa Jnana Yoga know that to be Yoga Karma Yoga O Arjuna For no one among Karma Yogins whose delusive identification of the body with the self has not been abandoned becomes a true Karma Yogin
2. That which they call monasticism know that to be Yoga O Pandava for nobody who has not given up expectations can be a yogi
3. Do you O Arjuna know that Yoga is what they call renunciation no one indeed becomes a Yogi who has not renounced their thoughts
4. What the learned call renunciation O son of Pandu know that to be the same as the Yoga For without renouncing intention for fruit one does not become a man of Yoga
5. O Arjuna renunciation is indeed what is known as right action No one can become spiritually enlightened who has not renounced all desires
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.3 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.3 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: आररकषरमनरयग करम करणमचयत यगरढसय तसयव शम करणमचयत 63
Translations:
1. Action is said to be the means for the sage who seeks to climb the heights of Yoga but when he has climbed the heights of Yoga tranquility is said to be the means
2. For the sage who wishes to ascend to Dhyana yoga action is said to be the means For that person once he has ascended to Dhyanayoga inaction alone is said to be the means
3. For a sage who wishes to attain to Yoga action is said to be the means for the same sage who has attained Yoga inaction is said to be the means
4. For a sage who is desirous of mounting upon the Yoga action is said to be the cause for the same sage when he has mounted upon the Yoga stillness is said to be the cause
5. For the sage who seeks the heights of spiritual meditation practice is the only method and when they have attained it they must maintain themselves there through continual selfcontrol
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.4 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.4 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यद ह ननदरयरथष न करमसवनषजजत सरवसङकलपसनयस यगरढसतदचयत 64
Translations:
1. For when one loses attachment to the things of the senses and to actions then they have abandoned all desires and are said to have climbed the heights of Yoga
2. Verily when a man who has given up thought about everything does not get attached to senseobjects or actions he is then said to be established in Yoga
3. When a person is not attached to the senseobjects or to actions having renounced all thoughts then they are said to have attained Yoga
4. When a person indulges neither in what is desired by the senses nor in the actions for it then being a man who has renounced all intentions he is said to have mounted on the path of Yoga
5. When a man renounces even the thought of initiating action and is not interested in sense objects or any results that may flow from his acts then he truly understands spirituality
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.5 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.5 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: उदधरदतमनऽऽतमन नतमनमवसदयत आतमव हयतमन बनधरतमव रपरतमन 65
Translations:
1. One should raise oneself by ones own mind and not allow oneself to sink for the mind alone is the friend of the self and the mind alone is the foe of the self
2. One should save oneself by oneself one should not degrade oneself For oneself is indeed ones own friend oneself is indeed ones own enemy
3. One should raise oneself by ones own self alone let not one lower oneself for the self alone is ones own friend and the self alone is ones own enemy
4. Let a person lift themselves by themselves and let them not depress themselves For the self alone is the friend of the self and the self alone is the foe of the self
5. Let him seek liberation with the help of his Highest Self and never disgrace his own Self For that Self is his only friend yet it can also be his enemy
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.6 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.6 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: बनधरतमऽऽतमनसतसय यनतमवतमन जत अनतमनसत शतरतव वरतततमव शतरवत 66
Translations:
1. The mind is the friend of him by whom it has been conquered But for him whose mind is not conquered the mind like an enemy remains hostile
2. He who has conquered his own self by the self his self is his own friend But for one who has not conquered his own self his self itself acts like an enemy
3. The Self is the friend of the self of him by whom the Self has been conquered but to the unconquered self this Self stands in the position of an enemy like an external foe
4. The Self is the friend of that Self by which the Self has been verily subdued but in the case of a person with an unsubdued Self the Self alone would abide in enmity like an enemy
5. To one who has conquered their lower nature with its help the Self is a friend but to one who has not done so it is an enemy
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.7 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.7 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: जततमन परशनतसय परमतम समहत शतषणसखदखष तथ मनपमनय 67
Translations:
1. Of him whose mind is conquered and who is serene the great self is wellsecured in heat and cold in pleasure and pain and in honor and dishonor

2. The supreme Self of one who has control over the aggregate of his body and organs and who is tranquil becomes manifest He should be poised in the midst of cold and heat happiness and sorrow as well as honor and dishonor
3. The Supreme Self of him who is selfcontrolled and peaceful remains balanced in cold and heat pleasure and pain as well as in honor and dishonor

4. The thinking of the person with a subdued mind and thus with complete calmness remains in equilibrium in the case of others and himself in cold and heat in pleasure and pain as well as in honor and dishonor
5. The Self of one who is selfcontrolled and has attained peace is equally unmoved by heat or cold pleasure or pain honor or dishonor
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.8 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.8 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: जञनवजञनतपततम कटसथ वजतनदरय यकत इतयचयत यग समलषटशमकञचन 68
Translations:

1. The yogin whose mind is content with knowledge of the self and also with knowledge of the difference between the self and Prakrti who is established in the self whose senses are subdued and to whom earth stone and gold all seem alike is called integrated
2. One whose mind is satisfied with knowledge and realization who is unmoved who has his organs under control is said to be Selfabsorbed The yogi treats all alike a lump of earth a stone and gold

3. The Yogi who is satisfied with the knowledge and wisdom of the Self who has conquered the senses and to whom a clod of earth a piece of stone and gold are all the same is said to have attained Nirvikalpa Samadhi

4. He whose self is satisfied with knowledge and with what consists of varied thoughts who remains peaklike and has completely subdued his senses and to whom a clod a stone and a piece of gold are the samethat man of Yoga is called a master of Yoga
5. He who desires nothing but wisdom and spiritual insight who has conquered his senses and who looks upon a lump of earth a stone and fine gold with the same eye is a real saint
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.9 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.9 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सहनमतररयदसनमधयसथदवषयबनधष सधषवप च पपष समबदधरवशषयत 69
Translations:
1. He who regards with an equal eye wellwishers friends foes the indifferent neutrals the hateful the relations and even the good and the sinfulhe excels
2. He excels who has sameness of view with regard to a benefactor a friend an enemy a neutral an arbiter the hateful a relative good people and even sinners
3. He who is of the same mind towards the goodhearted friends enemies the indifferent the neutral the hateful the relatives the righteous and the unrighteous excels
4. He whose mind is equal in the case of friend companion enemy the indifferent one the one who remains in the middle the foe the relative the righteous and also the sinfulhe excels all
5. He looks impartially on alllovers friends or foes indifferent hostile alien or relative virtuous or sinful
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.10 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.10 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यग यञजत सततमतमन रहस सथत एकक यतचतततम नरशरपरगरह 610
Translations:
1. The yogin should constantly fix his mind on yoga remaining in a solitary place all alone controlling his thoughts and mind free from desire and a sense of possession
2. A yogi should constantly concentrate his mind by staying in a solitary place alone with mind and body controlled free from expectations and free from acquisition
3. Let the yogi constantly strive to keep the mind steady remaining in solitude alone with the body and mind controlled and free from hope and greed
4. Let the man of Yoga always yoke the self mind by remaining alone in a lonely place controlling his mind and body without desire and without a sense of possession
5. Let the student of spirituality try unceasingly to concentrate his mind let him live in seclusion absolutely alone with his mind and personality controlled free from desire and without possessions
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.11 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.11 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शच दश परतषठपय सथरमसनमतमन नतयचछरत नतनच चलजनकशततरम 611
Translations:
1. Having established for himself in a clean spot a firm seat that is neither too high nor too low and covering it with cloth deerskin and Kusa grass in reverse order
2. Having firmly established in a clean place his seat neither too high nor too low composed of cloth skin and kusagrass placed successively one below the other
3. In a clean spot having established a firm seat of his own neither too high nor too low made of cloth skin and kusha grass layered one over the other
4. Setting up in a clean place his own suitable firm seat predominantly made of cloth skin and kusagrass neither too high nor too low for him
5. Having chosen a holy place let him sit in a firm posture on a seat that is neither too high nor too low and cover it with a grass mat a deer skin and a cloth
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.12 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.12 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: ततरकगर मन कतव यतचततनदरयकरय उपवशयसन यञजयदयगमतमवशदधय 612
Translations:
1. Sitting on the seat with a concentrated mind controlling the mind and senses one should practice Yoga for the purification of the self
2. Sitting on that seat he should concentrate his mind for the purification of the internal organ making the mind onepointed and keeping the actions of the mind and senses under control
3. There having made the mind onepointed with the actions of the mind and senses controlled let him seated on the seat practice Yoga for the purification of the self
4. Sitting there on the seat and making the mind singlepointed let him with the activities of his mind and senses subdued practice Yoga for selfpurification
5. Seated thus with his mind concentrated his functions controlled and his senses governed let him practice meditation for the purification of his lower nature
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.13 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.13 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सम कयशरगरव धरयननचल सथर सपरकषय नसकगर सव दशशचनवलकयन 613
Translations:
1. Holding the trunk head and neck erect motionless and steady gazing at the tip of the nose and not looking in any direction
2. Holding the body head and neck erect and still being steady looking at the tip of his own nose and not looking around
3. Let him firmly hold his body head and neck erect and still gazing at the tip of his nose without looking around
4. Holding the body head and neck erect and motionless remaining firm looking properly at ones own nosetip and not looking in different directions
5. Let him hold his body head and neck erect motionless and steady let him look fixedly at the tip of his nose turning neither to the right nor to the left
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.14 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.14 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: परशनततम वगतभरबरहमचरवरत सथत मन सयमय मचचतत यकत आसत मतपर 614
Translations:
1. Serene and fearless firm in their vow of celibacy controlling their minds and fixing their thoughts on Me they should sit in Yoga intent on Me
2. He should remain seated with a placid mind free from fear firm in the vow of celibacy and with the mind fixed on Me by controlling it through concentration having Me as the supreme goal
3. Sereneminded fearless firm in the vow of a Brahmachari having controlled their mind thinking of Me and balanced in mind let them sit having Me as their supreme goal
4. Being calmminded fearless and firmly devoted to celibacy controlling the mind completely let the master of Yoga remain fixing his mind on Me and having Me as his supreme goal
5. With peace in his heart and no fear observing the vow of celibacy with his mind controlled and fixed on Me let the student lose himself in contemplation of Me
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.15 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.15 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यञजननव सदऽऽतमन यग नयतमनस शनत नरवणपरम मतससथमधगचछत 615
Translations:
1. Applying his mind in this way the Yogin of controlled mind ever attains the peace which is the summit of beatitude and which abides in Me
2. Concentrating the mind thus forever the yogi of a controlled mind achieves the peace that culminates in liberation and abides in Me
3. Thus always keeping the mind balanced the yogi with the mind controlled attains the peace abiding in Me culminating in liberation
4. Yoking his mind incessantly in this manner My devotee with their mind not attached to anything else realizes peace which culminates in nirvana and ends in Me
5. Thus keeping his mind always in communion with Me and subduing his thoughts he shall attain that Peace which is Mine and will lead him to liberation at last
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.16 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.16 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: नतयशनतसत यगऽसत न चकनतमनशनत न चतसवपनशलसय जगरत नव चरजन 616
Translations:
1. Yoga is not for him who overeats nor for him who fasts excessively not for him O Arjuna who sleeps too much nor for him who stays awake too long
2. But O Arjuna Yoga is not for one who eats excessively nor for one who does not eat at all neither for one who habitually sleeps too long nor for one who stays awake
3. Verily Yoga is not possible for him who eats too much nor for him who does not eat at all nor for him who sleeps too much nor for him who is always awake O Arjuna
4. Yoga is neither for him who eats too much nor for him who totally abstains from eating nor for him who is prone to sleeping too much nor for him who keeps awake too much
5. Meditation is not for him who eats too much nor for him who eats not at all not for him who is overly addicted to sleep nor for him who is always awake
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.17 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.17 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यकतहरवहरसय यकतचषटसय करमस यकतसवपनवबधसय यग भवत दखह 617
Translations:
1. Yoga becomes the destroyer of sorrows for those who are temperate in food and recreation temperate in their actions and temperate in their sleep and wakefulness
2. Yoga becomes a destroyer of sorrow for one whose eating and movements are regulated whose effort in works is moderate and whose sleep and wakefulness are temperate
3. Yoga becomes the destroyer of pain for him who is moderate in eating and recreation such as walking etc who exercises moderation in action and who is moderate in sleep and wakefulness
4. The Yoga becomes a miserykiller for him whose effort for food is appropriate exertion in activities is proper and whose sleep and waking are proportionate
5. But for him who regulates his food and recreation who is balanced in action in sleep and in waking it shall dispel all unhappiness
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.18 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.18 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यद वनयत चततमतमनयववतषठत नसपह सरवकमभय यकत इतयचयत तद 618
Translations:
1. When the subdued mind rests solely on the Self then free from all yearning for objects of desire one is said to be fit for Yoga
2. A person who has become free from hankering for all desirable objects is said to be Selfabsorbed when the controlled mind rests solely in the Self
3. When the perfectly controlled mind rests in the Self alone free from longing for any of the objects of desire then it is said He is united
4. When his mind wellcontrolled is established in nothing but the Self and he is free from craving for any desired objectat that time he is called a master of Yoga
5. When the mind is completely controlled centered in the Self and free from all earthly desires then the man is truly spiritual
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.19 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.19 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यथ दप नवतसथ नङगत सपम समत यगन यतचततसय यञजत यगमतमन 619
Translations:
1. A lamp does not flicker in a windless placethat is the simile used for the tranquil mind of a yogi who practices yoga
2. As a lamp kept in a windless place does not flicker so is the simile for the yogi whose mind is under control and who is engaged in concentration on the Self
3. As a lamp placed in a windless spot does not flicker so is the Yogi of a controlled mind who practices Yoga in the Self compared
4. Just as a lamp in a windless place does not shake this simile is recalled in the case of the man of Yoga with a subdued mind practicing Yoga in the Self
5. The wise man who has conquered his mind and is absorbed in the Self is like a lamp that does not flicker as it stands sheltered from every wind
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.20 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.20 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यतरपरमत चतत नरदध यगसवय यतर चवतमनऽऽतमन पशयननतमन तषयत 620
Translations:
1. Where the mind controlled through the practice of Yoga rests and where one sees the Self by the Self and is delighted by the Self alone
2. At the time when the mind restrained through the practice of Yoga is withdrawn and just when one sees the Self by the self and remains contented in the Self alone
3. When the mind restrained by the practice of yoga attains quietude and when one sees the Self by the Self they are satisfied in their own Self
4. Where the mind restrained through yoga practice remains still and where observing the yogi finds satisfaction in the Self alone by perceiving only the Self within

5. There where the whole of nature is seen in the light of the Self where the person abides within their Self and is satisfied there their functions restrained by their union with the Divine the mind finds rest
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.21 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.21 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सखमतयनतक यततदबदधगरहयमतनदरयम वतत यतर न चवय सथतशचलत तततवत 621
Translations:
1. Where one knows that infinite happiness which can be grasped by the intellect but is beyond the reach of the senses and is established in that condition one does not swerve from it
2. When one experiences that absolute Bliss which can be intuited by the intellect and which is beyond the senses and being thus established this person surely does not swerve from Reality
3. When he the Yogi feels that infinite bliss which can be grasped by the pure intellect and which transcends the senses and is established therein never moving away from the reality
4. Where he realizes the limitless Bliss which is to be grasped by intellect and is beyond the senses remaining where he does not depart from the Reality
5. When he enjoys the bliss that surpasses the senses and can only be grasped by the pure intellect when he comes to rest within his highest self he will never again stray from reality
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.22 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.22 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: य लबधव चपर लभ मनयत नधक तत यसमनसथत न दखन गरणप वचलयत 622
Translations:
1. Having gained it one holds that there is no greater gain being established in it one is not moved even by the heaviest sorrow
2. Obtaining which one does not consider any other acquisition to be superior and being established in which one is not perturbed even by great sorrow
3. Having obtained it he thinks there is no other gain superior to it established in it he is not moved even by heavy sorrow
4. And having attained it he does not think of any other gain as superior to that being established in it he is not shaken much by misery however powerful it may be
5. Finding That he will realize that there is no possession so precious And once established there no calamity can disturb him
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.23 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.23 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: त वदयद दखसयगवयग यगसजञतम स नशचयन यकतवय यगऽनरवणणचतस 623
Translations:
1. Know this deliverance from association with misery to be Yoga This Yoga must be practiced with determination and with a mind free from despondency
2. One should know that severance of contact with sorrow to be what is called Yoga That Yoga should be practiced with perseverance and with an undepressed heart
3. Let this be known by the name of Yoga the severance from union with pain This Yoga should be practiced with determination and with an undespairing mind

4. That he would realize to be the cause for his cessation of contact with misery and to be the one made known by Yoga With determination that is to be yoked in Yoga by a person of undepressed mind or of the depressed mind
5. This inner severance from the affliction of misery is spirituality It should be practiced with determination and with a heart that refuses to be depressed
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.24 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.24 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: सङकलपपरभवनकमसतयकतव सरवनशषत मनसवनदरयगरम वनयमय समनतत 624
Translations:
1. Renouncing entirely all desires born of volition and restraining the mind from all the senses on all sides
2. By totally eschewing all desires that arise from thoughts and restraining all the organs with the mind from every side
3. Abandoning unreservedly all desires born of Sankalpa thought and imagination and completely restraining the whole group of senses by the mind from all sides
4. In order to renounce completely all desires that are born of intention let a person restrain the group of senseorgans from all sides by mind alone
5. Renouncing every desire that can be imagined controlling the senses at every point through the power of the mind
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.25 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.25 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: शन शनरपरमद बदधय धतगहतय आतमससथ मन कतव न कञचदप चनतयत 625
Translations:

1. Little by little one should withdraw oneself from objects other than the self with the help of the intellect held by firm resolution and then one should think of nothing else having fixed the mind upon the Self
2. One should gradually withdraw with the intellect endowed with steadiness Making the mind fixed in the Self one should not think of anything at all
3. Little by little let him attain steadiness of the intellect by holding it firmly having made the mind establish itself in the Self let him not think of anything else
4. Very slowly remain still keeping the mind well established in the Self by means of the intellect held in steadiness and let him not think of anything object
5. Little by little with the help of his reason controlled by fortitude let him attain peace and fixing his mind on the Self let him not think of anything else
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.26 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.26 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: यत यत नशचरत मनशचञचलमसथरम ततसतत नयमयतदतमनयव वश नयत 626
Translations:
1. Wherever the fickle and unsteady mind wanders one should subdue it and then bring it back under the control of the self alone
2. The yogi should bring this mind under the subjugation of the Self itself restraining it from all those causes that cause the restless unsteady mind to wander away
3. From whatever cause the restless and unsteady mind wanders away let him restrain it from that and bring it under the control of the Self alone
4. By whatever things the shaky and unsteady mind strays let him restrain it from those things and bring it back under the control of the Self alone
5. When the volatile and wavering mind wanders let him restrain it and bring it back to its allegiance to the Self
<s>[INST] Provide the meaning and context of Bhagavad Gita verse 6.27 spoken by भगवान. [/INST] Verse 6.27 of the Bhagavad Gita, spoken by भगवान, is as follows:
Sanskrit: परशनतमनस हयन यगन सखमततमम उपत शनतरजस बरहमभतमकलमषम 627
Translations:
1. For supreme happiness comes to the Yogin whose mind is at peace who is free from evil from whom Rajas has departed and who has become the Brahman
2. Supreme Bliss comes to this yogi alone whose mind has become perfectly tranquil whose quality of rajas has been eliminated who has become identified with Brahman and is untainted
3. Supreme Bliss indeed comes to this Yogi whose mind is made peaceful whose passion is quelled who has become Brahman and who is free from sin
4. Indeed the Supreme Bliss comes to this highly tranquilminded man of Yoga whose passions remain stilled who has become one with the Brahman and who is free from sins
